behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them
behaviors.justification: The grouping logic is a deterministic transformation: given a list of log entries with trace IDs, group by trace ID. This can be tested with in-memory test data representing entries from different services. A unit test that provides entries with matching trace IDs and verifies they're grouped correctly will catch real failures like incorrect string comparison, null handling, or deduplication bugs. The phrase 'regardless of which microservice' describes a property of the algorithm (trace ID is the only grouping key), not a requirement to test against real services.
behaviors.test_description: Create test entries with identical trace IDs from different mock service names. Verify: (1) entries are grouped into a single group, (2) all entries are present in the group, (3) order within the group is preserved. Test edge cases: empty trace IDs, null values, duplicate trace IDs, mixed case matching.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services
behaviors.justification: Clock skew handling is a pure algorithmic problem: given log entries with timestamps that may differ by up to 500ms due to clock misalignment, produce a correctly ordered timeline. A unit test can inject known timestamp deltas and verify the output ordering is correct. Real failures (incorrect comparison, off-by-one errors at boundaries, unstable sort) will be caught. The test doesn't need real services or real clocks—it needs synthetic entries with known relative timing to verify the ordering and skew compensation logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create entries with timestamps that differ by 100ms, 250ms, and 500ms (boundary cases). Verify: (1) output is sorted by logical timestamp, (2) entries within 500ms tolerance are correctly ordered, (3) sorting is stable (entries with same adjusted timestamp maintain insertion order). Test edge case: timestamps exactly at 500ms boundary.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report
behaviors.justification: Anomaly detection is a statistics problem: compute p99 latency from a dataset, compare against a baseline, flag if spike detected. A unit test can provide latency data with known p99 values and verify the comparison logic. Real failures (incorrect percentile calculation, off-by-one errors in threshold, missing flag output) will be caught at the unit level. The test doesn't need a persistent baseline store or real request traffic—only test data with synthetic latencies.
behaviors.test_description: Create test latency datasets: (1) baseline with known p99=100ms, then spiking to p99=350ms (3.5x), verify spike is flagged; (2) p99=299ms (2.99x, just below threshold), verify not flagged; (3) p99=300ms (exactly 3x), verify flagged. Verify the anomaly report includes spike metadata (severity, threshold crossed, actual p99 value).

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported
behaviors.justification: Error handling often spans multiple pipeline stages (read → parse → filter → aggregate). While parsing logic can be unit-tested, real failures emerge from integration: (1) if one stage fails to propagate skip status to the next, entries aren't counted; (2) if the counter is maintained at the wrong scope, concurrent or batch processing loses counts; (3) if error handling in one stage masks errors in another, crashes occur unexpectedly. A unit test of just the parser catches simple JSON syntax errors, but misses bugs in how errors are threaded through the pipeline and counted correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a full pipeline with mixed valid and malformed JSON entries (missing commas, unescaped quotes, truncated objects, non-JSON text). Verify: (1) pipeline processes without crashing, (2) valid entries are processed, (3) count of skipped malformed entries matches the number of bad entries injected, (4) count is accurate even with interleaved good/bad entries, (5) error handling doesn't lose entries in batches.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time throughput and resource constraint test. Failures occur at the system level: (1) OS process scheduling starves the tool under load, causing buffer overflow and entry loss; (2) I/O subsystem (file read, network recv) can't keep up, entries are dropped at the source; (3) memory pressure or garbage collection pauses cause the tool to fall behind. A unit or integration test with in-memory data might run fast enough to hide these failures. A system test requires actual streaming load, measuring latency under real resource contention, and verifying entry loss doesn't occur under peak load.
behaviors.test_description: Generate or stream 10,000 log entries at realistic rates (burst of 10k/sec or sustained high rate). Monitor: (1) tool accepts all 10,000 entries without dropping, (2) processing keeps pace (latency remains under acceptable threshold), (3) no entries are lost in queues or buffers, (4) memory usage stays within bounds, (5) tool doesn't crash or hang under sustained load. Verify by counting output entries and comparing against input.
